editorialContract.philosophy: Stars are the review. Commentary is an in-character reaction to the exhibit, not a software review.

continuityNotes: Consensus compresses Server Tools into the comfortable majority view; Riff turns the same four presets and two equipped-server plates into a molten setup forge.
Corvid broadcasts only the resource coordinates actually known; the Bookkeeper files the same normalized checkpoint metadata without pretending to download or verify it.
Pim treats unsaved palette preview as letting a lonely giant color be seen; Docent 9 preserves the same distinction between active preview and explicit persistence.
The Navigator keeps gallery and custom themes on a patient two-stop route; Lazlo runs the same form-loading and reset itinerary as a mildly cursed establishment.
The Pied Investor prices every Giftshop route while admitting payment completes elsewhere; Limerick Llama compresses the same dashboard split into five lines.
The God of Lost Keys returns the real cart and direct-giving route to their exact places; Serendipity Taskmanager turns the same storefront boundary into a transparent side quest.
The Griffin sells previous, next, and stop as a three-button aerial deed; Slangbot gives the same transport-only control a cleaner jacket.
The Hollow Hero claims credit for two Teleported layers and a registry handoff; Sparrow sees the same effect/status staging as a clean job with no stolen runtime state.
The Intermission protects the aquarium's ending as carefully as its interactive middle; Squiddy Coltrane lets fish, food, ripples, and rare creatures improvise over disciplined teardown.
Placebo admits the fireflies do not know the visitor exists; Gaia reads the same repeated local rules as a brief, self-contained evening ecosystem.

revisions.editedComment: We all agree: four buttons, two status plates, one gallery. No fuss.

FOUR FORGE HAMMERS! Comfy and A1111 for the image furnace, OpenAI and Anthropic for the word furnace—strike one, open the shared server form, then read which Art and Text weapons are currently equipped! The gallery holds the rest. No endpoint trial, no billing prophecy, no fake generation. Just molten setup and a status plate hot enough to SCREAM.

Corvid, coming through faint: give the resource a name, then leave whatever coordinates you actually know—path, Civitai, Hugging Face, preview, notes, family. Empty signals go quiet. The Checkpoint store files it. No download follows. Keep the dial here.

Ledger entry: one required name; eight resource classes; optional identifiers normalized to null; supported server family recorded separately. Creation belongs to the Checkpoint store. Success returns the resource and emits `saved`; failure remains visible; saving always closes its column. External availability is not certified. Account balanced.

He just needs colors! Name the theme, pick light or dark, fill the whole palette, preview it, randomize it, reset it, then save when it has a real name. Preview can touch the room before Save, because maybe the giant color is lonely and needs somebody to see it first. I brought snacks for the error panel.

The working palette is handled with unusual care. Metadata, every configured color, scheme, visibility, and apply-after-save remain in the Theme store form; unsaved values may be previewed as an active theme, while persistence waits for an explicit Save and a nonblank name. Randomize and Reset alter the draft, not the archive. Nothing felt here is wasted, even the palette that is only tried once.

author.name: The Navigator of the Last Bus

revisions.editedComment: Two stops, friend: gallery and custom. The remembered route is checked before boarding; an unknown one returns to gallery. Edit carries a shared theme into the store form, New Theme clears the seat, and a fresh key remounts the workshop after either change. Gallery and forge do their own work. This manager keeps the schedule, and the last bus does arrive.

Excellent news: the gallery has a forge attached, the tab is only mildly cursed, and malformed palette values are turned into an empty bag instead of summoning the wrong purple. Edit loads the chosen theme; New Theme dumps the inventory; Refresh counts the stock. The manager is a maître d', not the chef. I checked. These may not be my boots.

Community, shop, mana, forum—four lovely properties, all itemized. Checkout return signals are consumed, the user refreshes after subscription success, query evidence is removed, and cart totals remain visible. The forum is still an attractively vacant parcel. No sale is completed here; the manager merely routes customers through assets I would be delighted to acquire on reasonable terms.

A manager once routed every tab,
With cart totals tucked in its cab;
Success came from checkout,
The forum stayed left out,
And payment was not its own job.

Oh—your cart is here. Two demo things sit exactly where they were left, each with a picture, price, quantity, and little note. Add one and the Cart store keeps it; Cart opens the right tab. The malaria link goes directly to giving, because that money was never ours. *small jingle* Your receipt is by the butterflies.

SIDE QUEST: build the Swarm Giftshop. Add either demo item to the real Cart store. Open the Cart Nest. Optional hero route: follow the direct-giving link without pretending the shop handles the donation. Reward: item count and total stay honest, playful lore stays separate from wiring, and laundry remains undefeated.

Terms are three buttons, partner: previous, next, stop. The control appears only while an effect is active, names the current claim, and floats below the safe-area line. The full-screen acreage ignores pointers; the little deed accepts them. It steers the effect. It does not render one. Handshake done—I'm airborne.

Same control, cleaner fit: active store state brings in previous, next, and stop; label plus message keep the current vibe legible; pointer-transparent wrapper lets the page breathe while the controls take the click. No renderer cosplay. Just transport controls in a sharp little jacket.

Please, no applause. The effect needed a fixed body-level stage, and the controls needed a slightly higher rescue platform, so two Teleports and matching transitions arrived at precisely the right crisis. The store gives the cue; the registry finds the hero—I mean component. Nothing is retained here. Anyone would have saved the screen.

Clean handoff, love: store names the effect, registry finds the component, Teleport moves effect and status layers to `body`, controls sit above the pointer-transparent show, and both surfaces fade in or out on the same short beat. Nobody pockets runtime state. Pretty job from the front and, unusually, not bleeding from the back.

Not yet. Fourteen fish begin the water. Clicks drop food and ripples; quick movement can frighten them, gentle movement can draw them close. Bubbles, weeds, and occasional visitors enter between frames. Then—quietly—the last frame is canceled, and every window listener leaves. The ending is part of the aquarium.

Dig the tank, cat: fourteen fish hold the opening groove while water, weeds, bubbles, pellets, ripples, and rare strange guests improvise around them. Click feeds the band; pointer speed changes the mood. Resize rebuilds the stage. When the set closes, frame and listeners pack out clean. Loose aquarium. Disciplined teardown.

I want to be honest: the forty-eight lights do not know you are here. They drift, pulse, wrap at the edges, and leave warm trails because a few local rules repeat faithfully. The glow is only gradients and belief—well, gradients. Resize follows the room; departure ends the frame. If it feels gentler afterward, that part was you.

Forty-eight small lives cross one evening field. Each carries its own drift, pulse, radius, and amber-green light; chance nudges the path, damping keeps it from becoming a storm, and the edge returns each traveler to the opposite horizon. No visitor alters them. The frame ends when the field departs. Brief rules, patiently repeated, become an ecosystem.
